---
#title: "Chapter 4: Non-linear mixed effects models"
#author: "Dagan Lonsdale"
#bibliography: chapter4.bib
#csl: aa.csl

#output:
#  bookdown::html_document2:
#    fig_caption : TRUE
#    number_sections: no
#    toc: yes
#    toc_float: yes
#    latex_engine: xelatex
#    highlight: tango
#    extra_dependencies: "subfig"
#header-includes:
#  - \usepackage{float}
#  - \floatplacement{table}{H}
#  - \usepackage{booktabs}
#  - \usepackage{caption}
#  - \captionsetup{labelfont=bf,margin=12pt}
#urlcolor: blue

title: "Non-linear mixed effects models"
output:
  bookdown::html_document2:
    fig_caption : TRUE
    number_sections: no
    toc: yes
    toc_float: yes

header-includes:
  - \usepackage{colortbl}
  - \usepackage{multirow}
  - \usepackage{float}
  - \floatplacement{figure}{H}
  - \floatplacement{table}{H}
  - \usepackage{booktabs}
  - \usepackage{caption}
  - \captionsetup{labelfont=bf,margin=12pt} 
  - \newcommand{\blandscape}{\begin{landscape}}
  - \newcommand{\elandscape}{\end{landscape}}

---


```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, fig.pos= "H")
library(tidyverse)
library(kableExtra)
library(gridExtra)
library(PKNCA) 
library(RColorBrewer)
```

# Learning outcomes  

* Describe what a compartmental model adds over NCA
* Fit a one-compartment IV bolus model in nlmixr2
* Read a model parameter table and relate the estimates to the NCA values from Session 1

# Tasks

* Fit a one-compartment IV bolus model for the Healthy dataset
* Fit a one-compartment IV bolus model for the Sepsis dataset
* Compare volume of distribution and clearance values for the two datasets
* Compare volume of distribution and clearance values using non-compartmental and compartmental analysis

Extensions:
* Fit two-compartment models to the datasets
* Create diagnostic plots for your models (See Additional Materials)

# Introduction 

'Rich' data describes where there are lots of samples for each individual at each time point. This kind of data is really easy to produce in early-phase trials, including entry-into-human studies. 

However, at later phases of drug development, including post-marketing, pharmacokinetic data are often more challenging to obtain. In addition, rigid sampling times are less easy to implement outside of the confines of a dedicated pharmacokinetic study as patients or study participants are often at clinic or study visits at irregular times and so sampling may be somewhat opportunistic. Self-administered doses are also less likely to be accurately taken at prescribed times. These factors make modelling data using the non-compartmental methods we have used so far challenging and less accurate in these scenarios.

The purpose of this session, is to introduce the concept of non-linear mixed-effects modelling. This will include introduction to the modelling package <TT>nlmixr</TT>. This package is challenging to install on your own machine, <TT>install.packages('nlmixr2')</TT> may work. If you run a Windows operating system, you are likely to also need <TT>rtools</TT>. You will need to follow instructions online on how to install and download this. Installation on an Apple Mac usually requires <TT>Xcode command line tools</TT> to be downloaded. We recommend using Posit Cloud for this session. 

## Advantages and disadvantages of non-linear mixed-effects models

**Advantages of non-linear mixed-effects approach**

* Enables modelling of sparse data, useful in later phase studies
* Allows more complex identification of covariate effects (for example organ function)
* Alternative sampling and modelling may allow for populations to be studied that are not straightforward to study in traditional phase-0/1 design (for example children, or drugs in pregnancy)
* Allows complex simulations which can inform subsequent study design

**Disadvantages of non-linear mixed-effects approach**

* Feels inherently more complex which can make people anxious about *undertaking* or *understanding* the process
* Computationally expensive
* Time consuming
* Often requires time cleaning/organising data from multiple sources 

# The one-compartment IV-bolus model
Time concentration-time profile of the one-compartment IV-bolus model (Figure \@ref(fig:onecpt)) may be described using the equation $$C=C_0e^{(-k_et)}$$ where $k_e=\frac{Cl}{V_d}$ and $C_0=\frac{Dose}{V_d}$

```{r onecpt, echo = FALSE, message=FALSE, fig.align='center', fig.cap='One-compartment model',fig.height=2,out.width='0.6\\linewidth'}
knitr::include_graphics("onecpt1.png")
```

Taking the log of this equation provides us with $$log(C)=log(C_0)-k_et$$ which is an equation of a straight line, i.e. of the form $$y=mx+c$$ Data from a pharmacokinetic experiment may therefore be analysed using a compartmental approach and linear regression analysis, as calculating the gradient provides $k_e$ and the intercept provides $C_0$ from which $V_d$ may be calculated. 

Real world experiments do not generate 'clean' data, that produce nice smooth lines of best fit that pass through all the data points. There is additional (residual) variability. Let's look at an example from a single individual with a realistic time-concentration profile (Figure \@ref(fig:single)). 

```{r single,echo=F,eval=T,fig.cap="PK data from an individual", fig.subcap=c('Standard plot','Semi-log plot with linear regression'),fig.ncol = 2, out.width = "50%", fig.align = 'center', warning=F,message=F}
set.seed(2)
N<-1;P<-0.75;Q=1+6/P;ID<-rep(LETTERS[1:N],each=Q);time<-rep(seq(0,6,by=P),N)
mCl<-9.8;Cl<- mCl*exp(rep(rnorm(sd=0.4,n=N),each=Q))
mV<-12.0;V<-mV*exp(rep(rnorm(sd=0.1,n=N),each=Q))
ke<-Cl/V 
Dose<-40 
onoral<- function(t) (Dose/V)*exp(-(Cl/V)*t)
conc<- signif(onoral(time),2) 
error<-rnorm(mean=0,sd=0.3,n=length(conc))
conc<-conc+conc*error+rnorm(mean=0,sd=0.2,n=length(conc)) # additive and proportional residual error
fru<-data.frame(ID,time,conc)

ggplot(fru,aes(x=time,y=conc))+
  geom_point()+
  theme_bw(base_size=18)+
  theme(legend.position="none")+
  ylab("Plasma concentration (mg/L)")+
  xlab("Time (hr)")
ggplotRegression <- function (fit) {
require(ggplot2)
ggplot(fit$model, aes_string(x = names(fit$model)[2], y = names(fit$model)[1])) + 
  geom_point()+
  theme_bw(base_size=18)+
  theme(legend.position="none")+
  ylab("Log plasma concentration (mg/L)")+
  xlab("Time (hr)") +
  stat_smooth(method = "lm", col = "red",se=F,alpha=0.6)+
  annotate("text", x=4.2, y=1.4, hjust = 0,label=paste("C0 =",signif(fit$coef[[1]],2)))+
  annotate("text", x=4.2, y=1.2, hjust = 0,label=paste( "ke =",-signif(fit$coef[[2]], 2)))+
  annotate("text", x=4.2, y=1, hjust = 0,label=paste( "p =",signif(summary(fit)$coef[2,4], 2)))
  
}
ggplotRegression(lm(log(conc) ~ time, data = fru))
```

# Rich versus sparse data 
## Rich data example
Let's remind ourselves of what the output from a rich sampling schedule from a pharmacokinetic trial looks like. Figure \@ref(fig:rich) shows data from a simulated experiment of an orally administered 40mg dose of furosemide. There are 10 participants, each with a sample taken at 10 different time points. The fact that there are the same number of samples is important (it is known as *balanced* data). This reduces the possibility of one individual unduly influencing an analysis of data. 

```{r rich,echo=F,eval=T,fig.cap="Rich data from a simulated pharmacokinetic trial (n=10, 10 samples per participant)", fig.subcap=c('Concentration plot','Semi-log concentration plot'),fig.ncol = 2, out.width = "50%", fig.align = 'center', warning=F,message=F}

N<-10
P<-0.5
E<-6

time<-rep(c(0,0.1,0.5,0.75,1,2,3,4,5,6),N)
Q=length(time)/N
ID<-rep(LETTERS[1:N],each=Q) 

set.seed(2)
mCl<-9.8  
Cl<- mCl*exp(rep(rnorm(sd=0.4,n=N),each=Q))
mV<-12.0  
V<-mV*exp(rep(rnorm(sd=0.1,n=N),each=Q))

ke<-Cl/V 
Dose<-40 
mka<-1.3
ka<- mka*exp(rep(rnorm(sd=0.1,n=N),each=Q))
F<-0.65

onoral<- function(t) ((Dose*F*ka)/(V*(ka-ke)))*(exp(-ke*t)-exp(-ka*t))

conc<- signif(onoral(time),2) 
error<-rnorm(mean=0,sd=0.2,n=length(conc))
conc<-conc+conc*error
conc<-ifelse(conc<=0,0.001,conc)
fru<-data.frame(ID,time,conc)
frud<-fru %>% subset(time==0) %>% mutate(dose=40)


conc<-PKNCAconc(fru, conc~time|ID) #create sampling object
dose<-PKNCAdose(frud, dose~time|ID) #create dosing object
manual <- data.frame(start=0, end=8,vz.obs=TRUE,kel.last=TRUE, cl.obs=TRUE)
clvd<-PKNCAdata(conc,dose,intervals=manual)  #combine
results<-pk.nca(clvd)  # analyse
res<-results$result

v<-res %>% subset(PPTESTCD=='vz.obs')%>% summarise(v=mean(PPORRES)) %>% unlist()
cl<-res %>% subset(PPTESTCD=='cl.obs')%>% summarise(cl=mean(PPORRES)) %>% unlist()
rich<-ggplot(fru,aes(x=time,y=conc,group=ID,col=ID))+
  geom_line(alpha=0.2,col='grey')+
  geom_point()+
  theme_bw(base_size=18)+
  theme(legend.position="none")+
  ylab("Plasma concentration mg/L")+
  xlab("Time (hr)")
rich
rich+
  scale_y_continuous(trans='log2', breaks=c(0.01,1))
```

We have several options in our armoury that we can use to describe these data. We could undertake a non-compartmental approach (Table \@ref(tab:nca)) and use the <TT>PKNCA</TT> package. Or, we could fit a model to the data.  


```{r nca,   echo=F,eval=T}
summary(results) %>% 
    kable(booktabs = TRUE, caption = "Non compartmental analysis of oral PK data")
```

## Fitting a model
We can use our prior knowledge to choose a model to fit to the data that aligns with our knowledge of the system (human) that the data came from. In this case we will choose the one-compartment oral-absorption model. Note that there are 3 parameters to estimate in the one-compartment oral-absorption model - $k_e$ (or $Cl$), $V_d$, and $k_a$. Importantly, there are more samples per participant than there are model parameters. This indicates that we have *rich* data to model. 

```{r oneor, echo = FALSE, message=FALSE, fig.align='center', fig.cap='One-compartment oral-absorption model',fig.height=3,out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics("oneor2.png")
```

Figure \@ref(fig:oneor) shows the graphical representation of the one-compartment oral-absorption model. To remind you, the equation for this model is $$C=\frac{F.D.k_a}{V_d.(k_a-k_e)}.(e^{-k_et}-e^{-k_at}) $$ Remember, that without corresponding IV data, we cannot identify $F$. 

### Analytical apprach 
There are several ways to approach modelling these data: 

* Treat all the data as if it is from a single individual, pool it together and fit a model (naive pooled approach)
* Average the data at each time point and then fit a model (naive average approach)
* Fit a model to each individual and then take an average of these pharmacokinetic parameters (two-stage approach)

Each of these approaches provides a slightly different model fit and each has its own pro's and con's. If you would like to read more about these, I suggest taking a look at the excellent article by Ette & Williams @Ette.

Figure \@ref(fig:richfit) demonstrates the time-concentration profile modeled using each of these approaches. As the data was simulated, we also know the *true* population time-concentration profile. You can see that each of the different modeling approaches provides a slightly different fit to these data. None of them are perfect, but all provide an acceptable estimation of the true profile. They could all be used to make inferences from a pharmacokinetic study of this type.

```{r richfit,echo=F,eval=T,fig.cap="Concentration-time profile showing \`true\' population profile alonside a variety of approaches to modelling the rich data data", fig.subcap=c('Full plot','Focussed plot to demonstrate different profiles'),fig.ncol = 2, out.width = "50%", fig.align = 'center', warning=F,message=F}

#Naive pooled approach
npelim<-fru %>% 
  subset(time>=2) %>% 
  lm(log(conc)~time,data=.)
npI<-exp(coef(npelim)[1])
npke<--coef(npelim)[2]

npab<-fru %>% 
  subset(time<=0.75) %>% 
  mutate(diff=(npI*exp(-npke*time))-conc) %>% 
  lm(log(diff)~time,data=.)
npka<--coef(npab)[2]


#Naive pooled average approach
npa<-fru %>%
  group_by(time)%>%
  mutate(mean=mean(conc)) %>% 
  subset(time>=2) %>% 
  lm(log(mean)~time,data=.) 

npaI<-exp(coef(npa)[1])
npake<--coef(npa)[2]

npaab<-fru %>% 
  group_by(time)%>%
  mutate(mean=mean(conc)) %>% 
  distinct(time,.keep_all = TRUE) %>% 
  subset(time<=0.75) %>% 
  mutate(diff=(npaI*exp(-npake*time))-mean) %>% 
  lm(log(diff)~time,data=.)
npaka<--coef(npaab)[2]

# Two stage approach
library(broom)



fitted_models = fru %>% 
  group_by(ID) %>% 
  slice((Q-3):Q) %>% 
  ungroup() %>% 
  nest_by(ID) %>%
  mutate(fit = list(lm(log(conc)~time,data = data)))%>%
  summarise(tidy(fit)) 

tsI<-fitted_models %>% 
  group_by(term) %>% 
  subset(term=='(Intercept)') %>% 
  summarise(mean=exp(mean(estimate))) %>% 
  ungroup()%>% 
  pull(mean)
tske<-fitted_models %>% 
  group_by(term) %>% 
  subset(term=='time') %>% 
  summarise(mean=-mean(estimate)) %>% 
  ungroup()%>% 
  pull(mean)

frutm<-fru %>% 
  group_by(ID) %>% 
  filter(conc==max(conc)) %>% # filter the data.frame to keep row where x is maximum
  select(ID,time) %>%
  rename(timin=time) %>% 
  ungroup()
frutm<-merge(fru,frutm,all.x=T)

fitted_models = frutm %>% 
  group_by(ID) %>% 
  subset(time<timin) %>% 
  mutate(diff=(npI*exp(-npke*time))-conc)%>% 
  ungroup() %>% 
  nest_by(ID) %>%  
  mutate(fit = list(lm(log(diff)~time,data = data)))%>%
  summarise(tidy(fit)) 
tska<-fitted_models %>% 
  group_by(term) %>% 
  subset(term=='time') %>% 
  summarise(mean=-mean(estimate)) %>% 
  ungroup()%>% 
  pull(mean)


mke<-mCl/mV
sim<-function(x) ((Dose*F*mka)/(mV*(mka-mke)))*(exp(-mke*x)-exp(-mka*x))
np<- function(x) npI*(exp(-npke*x)-exp(-npka*x))
npa<-function(x) npaI*(exp(-npake*x)-exp(-npaka*x))
ts<-function(x) tsI*(exp(-tske*x)-exp(-tska*x))

time<-seq(0,6.1,by=0.1)
conc<-c(sim(time),np(time),npa(time),ts(time))
time<-rep(time,4)
data<-rep(c("True (population) PK profile","Naive pooled","Naive pooled average","Two-stage"),each=62)
data<-factor(data,levels=c("True (population) PK profile","Naive pooled","Naive pooled average","Two-stage"))

full<-data.frame(data,time,conc)

richfitplot<- ggplot()+
  geom_point(data=fru,aes(x=time,y=conc),alpha=0.6,col='grey')+
  geom_line(data=full,aes(x=time,y=conc,lty=data,col=data,alpha=data),size=1)+
  theme_bw(base_size=18)+
  theme(legend.position=c(0.72,0.8),
        legend.background = element_rect(fill = NA),
        legend.title=element_blank(),
        legend.text=element_text(size=14))+
  ylab("Plasma concentration mg/L")+
  xlab("Time (hr)")+
  #scale_linetype_manual(values=c("solid", "twodash","dotted","dotdash"))+
  scale_alpha_manual(name = "data", values = c(0.8,1,1,1))+
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Dark2")
richfitplot
richfitplot+
  scale_x_continuous( limits = c(1,3))+
  scale_y_continuous( limits = c(0.25,1.1))
```  
## Sparse data example
Let's look at what happens if the sampling is sparse (fewer samples) and less strictly taken at specified time points. 


```{r sparse,echo=F,eval=T,fig.cap="Sparse data from a simulated pharmacokinetic trial (n=40, 1-3 samples per participant)", fig.subcap=c('Concentration-time plot - lines are drawn to link some individuals to demonstrate that each individual provides few samples','Semi-log concentration-time plot'),fig.ncol = 2, out.width = "50%", fig.align = 'center', warning=F,message=F}

N<-40
set.seed(1)

R<-sample(1:3,N,TRUE)
ID<-rep(stringi::stri_rand_strings(N,3),R)


mCl<-9.8  # set mean Cl
Cl<- mCl*exp(rep(rnorm(sd=0.2,n=N),R))
mV<-12.0  # set mean V
V<-mV*exp(rep(rnorm(sd=0.2,n=N),R))
ke<-Cl/V 



timea = sample(x=c(0,0.1,0.5,0.75,1),size=sum(R)/3,TRUE)*exp(rnorm(sd=0.1,n=sum(R)/3))
timeb = sample(c(1,2,3,4),size=sum(R)/3,TRUE)*exp(rnorm(sd=0.1,n=sum(R)/3))
timec = sample(c(5,6),size=sum(R)/3,TRUE)*exp(rnorm(sd=0.1,n=sum(R)/3))

time<-c(rbind(timea,timeb,timec))
time<-if(length(time)==length(ID)) time else sample(x=time,size=sum(R),replace = TRUE)

Dose<-40 # 40 mg dose is same for each patient
mka<-1.3
ka<- mka*exp(rep(rnorm(sd=0.1,n=N),R))
F<-0.65
onoral<-((Dose*F*ka)/(V*(ka-ke)))*(exp(-ke*time)-exp(-ka*time))
b<-data.frame(ka,ke)
error<-rnorm(mean=0,sd=0.2,n=length(onoral))
#onoral<-onoral+onoral*error
conc<- signif(onoral,2) 
fru<-data.frame(ID,time,conc)
fru<-arrange(fru, ID, time)
library(RColorBrewer)
nb.cols <- length(unique(ID))
mycolors <- colorRampPalette(brewer.pal(8, "Dark2"))(nb.cols)
sparse<-ggplot(fru,aes(x=time,y=conc,group=ID,col=ID))+
  geom_point()+
  theme_bw(base_size=18)+
  theme(legend.position="none")+
  ylab("Plasma concentration mg/L")+
  xlab("Time (hr)")
sparse+
  geom_line(data=fru %>% 
    filter(ID %in% sample(unique(ID),10)),alpha=0.5)
sparse+
  scale_y_continuous(trans='log2', breaks=c(0.01,1))
```
Figure \@ref(fig:sparse) shows a simulation of data that might more realistically be drawn from a later phase clinical trial. You can see that there are quite a number of participants (`r N`) but that they do not contribute many samples. The shape of the data is the same (because the underlying PK parameters used to simulate the data are the same as I used in simulating the rich example). However, here one or two participants contribute 3-samples, but the majority have just 1-2 samples. The fact that there are different numbers of samples per participant is described as *unbalanced*.  We therefore have fewer samples per participants than there are parameters to estimate in the model (sparse data). You can also see that samples were not taken at precisely the same time-points after a dose. 

Figure \@ref(fig:sparsefit) shows the same analytical approaches to these data as we used before with the rich data. The two-stage approach falls down entirely because most individuals don't have enough samples to correctly determine absorption and elimination phases. The naive-pooled-average approach is equally unhelpful because samples were taken at non-uniform times so averaging out is inaccurate and leads to a poor model fit. 

The naive-pooled approach still gives a pretty good fit, primarily because there are so many samples. However, whilst the average trend is good, the problem with a naive pooled approach, is that it *overestimates* variability. Since we treat all the data as if it comes from one individual, all the variability is dumped into a single residual error term at the end of the equation. $$C=\frac{F.D.k_a}{V_d.(k_a-k_e)}.(e^{-k_et}-e^{-k_at})+\epsilon$$ By lumping all of the residual error into this single error term, the naive-pooled modelling approach misses out on describing accurately the time-concentration profile of an individual. This is fine if you just care about the mean-concentration, but we often care about what is happening to the whole population (or at least most of it). 


```{r sparsefit,echo=F,eval=T,fig.cap="Concentration-time profile showing \'true\' population profile alonside a variety of approaches to modelling sparse data",fig.width=6,fig.height=3,fig.align = 'center',warning=FALSE,message=FALSE}

#Naive pooled approach
npelim<-fru %>% 
  subset(time>=2) %>% 
  lm(log(conc)~time,data=.)
npI<-exp(coef(npelim)[1])
npke<--coef(npelim)[2]

npab<-fru %>% 
  subset(time<=0.75) %>% 
  mutate(diff=(npI*exp(-npke*time))-conc) %>% 
  lm(log(diff)~time,data=.)
npka<--coef(npab)[2]


#Naive pooled average approach


npa<-fru %>%
  group_by(interval = round(fru$time/4)*4) %>%
  summarize(mean= mean(conc)) %>% 
  subset(interval>=2)%>% 
  lm(log(mean)~interval,data=.) 
npaI<-exp(coef(npa)[1])
npake<--coef(npa)[2]

npaab<-fru %>% 
  group_by(time = round(fru$time/0.5)*0.5) %>%
  summarize(mean= mean(conc)) %>% 
  subset(time<=3) %>% 
  mutate(diff=(npaI*exp(-npake*time))-mean) %>% 
  subset(diff>0) %>%
  lm(log(diff)~time,data=.)
npaka<--coef(npaab)[2]

# Two stage approach
library(broom)
fitted_models = fru %>% 
  subset(time>=2) %>% 
  nest_by(ID) %>%
  mutate(fit = list(lm(log(conc)~time,data = data)))%>%
  summarise(tidy(fit)) 

tsI<-fitted_models %>% 
  group_by(term) %>% 
  subset(term=='(Intercept)') %>% 
  summarise(mean=exp(mean(estimate))) %>% 
  ungroup()%>% 
  pull(mean)
tske<-fitted_models %>% 
  group_by(term) %>% 
  subset(term=='time') %>% 
  summarise(mean=-mean(estimate,na.rm=TRUE)) %>% 
  ungroup()%>% 
  pull(mean)

frutm<-fru %>% 
  group_by(ID) %>% 
  filter(conc==max(conc)) %>% # filter the data.frame to keep row where x is maximum
  select(ID,time) %>%
  rename(timin=time) %>% 
  ungroup()
frutm<-merge(fru,frutm,all.x=T)

fitted_models = frutm %>% 
  group_by(ID) %>% 
  subset(time<=timin)%>% 
  filter(n_distinct(conc) > 1) %>% 
  mutate(diff=(npI*exp(-npke*time))-conc)%>% 
  ungroup() %>%
  subset(diff>0) %>% 
  nest_by(ID) %>%  
  mutate(fit = list(lm(log(diff)~time,data = data)))%>%
  summarise(tidy(fit)) 

tska<-fitted_models %>% 
  group_by(term) %>% 
  subset(term=='time') %>% 
  summarise(mean=-mean(estimate,na.rm=TRUE)) %>% 
  ungroup()%>% 
  pull(mean)

mke<-mCl/mV
sim<-function(x) ((Dose*F*mka)/(mV*(mka-mke)))*(exp(-mke*x)-exp(-mka*x))
np<- function(x) npI*(exp(-npke*x)-exp(-npka*x))
npa<-function(x) npaI*(exp(-npake*x)-exp(-npaka*x))
ts<-function(x) tsI*(exp(-tske*x)-exp(-tska*x))


time<-seq(0,8,by=0.1)
conc<-c(sim(time),np(time),npa(time),ts(time))
time<-rep(seq(0,8,by=0.1),4)
data<-rep(c("True (population) PK profile","Naive pooled","Naive pooled average","Two-stage"),each=81)
data<-factor(data,levels=c("True (population) PK profile","Naive pooled","Naive pooled average","Two-stage"))

full<-data.frame(data,time,conc)


ggplot()+
  geom_point(data=fru,aes(x=time,y=conc),alpha=0.6,col='grey')+
  geom_line(data=full,aes(x=time,y=conc,lty=data,col=data,alpha=data),size=1)+
  theme_bw(base_size=9)+
  theme(legend.position=c(0.75,0.8),
        legend.background = element_rect(fill = NA),
        legend.title=element_blank(),
        legend.text=element_text(size=10))+
  ylab("Plasma concentration mg/L")+
  xlab("Time (hr)")+
  scale_linetype_manual(values=c("solid", "twodash","dotted","dotdash"))+
  scale_alpha_manual(name = "data", values = c(0.8,1,1,1))+
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Dark2")


```


# Non-linear, mixed-effects modelling, an alternative approach

Before we dip our toes into the water of population-pharmacokinetic modelling using non-linear mixed-effects techniques, we must briefly note a few definitions. 

## Linear versus non-linear models

Linear models are (relatively) easy to solve and calculate estimates for model parameters. Linear transformations are not always possible. When undertaking a non-linear approach, computer programs use an *iterative approach* to finding values for model parameters. With an iterative approach, a series of values for model parameters are tested to see which provide the values for model parameters that best fit the data. 

The biggest difference you will notice in your introduction to non-linear modelling is that you will now be required to input an *initial estimate* for model parameters, i.e. you will have to give <TT>R</TT> a value for clearance and volume of distribution that you want it to start at in its search for its final estimate of the population value of that parameter. That's right, you give the program your best guess of what the true population value is and then the program tries to improve on it. 

## Fixed-effects, random-effects and mixed-effects models
Fixed-effects in an experiment or trial are controlled, or the same, for each individual. For example, drug dose and frequency of administration are usually fixed. They may also be variables where all possible values/levels are studied. For example, if you want to study the effect of smoking on the kinetics of a drug, you would study the drug kinetics in smokers- and non-smokers. The 'smoking' effect in your model is a fixed-effect since all levels of this factor (categorical)-variable are studied. 

Random-effects in an experiment or trial are less controlled and do not include all possible levels. For example, we take a sample of participants in a trial, but we really want to know/make inferences about the population from which they are sampled. Since we do not study every single subject from the population, the participants in the study are a random effect. 

Mixed-effects is the easiest to define here. It is simply a model which contains fixed- and random-effects. 

### Using a compartmental pharmacokinetic model to provide an example of what we mean
Let's return to the one-compartment IV-bolus model once more. Let us parameterise using *primary* pharmacokinetic parameters (clearance and volume of distribution). Broadly, our model remains the same $$C=C_{0}e^{-\frac{Cl}{V}t}$$ however, we now add a further layer, by stating that our parameters are made up of a fixed (population mean) effect and a random (inter-individual variability) effect. $$Cl=\theta_{Cl}\times e^{\eta_{Cl}}$$ the modelling process then estimates $$\theta_{Cl}\textrm{ -the population mean value for clearance (a fixed effect)}$$ and makes some estimate of $$\eta_{Cl} \textrm{ -individual variability of clearance (a random-effect)}$$

Similarly, we model volume of distribution as $$V=\theta_{V}\times e^{\eta_{V}}$$ and by extension in the oral-absorption model $$k_a=\theta_{k_a}\times e^{\eta_{k_a}}$$

### A further note on $\eta$

Let's imagine an individual in a clinical trial, lets call them individual $i$, or the $i$th individual. For $i$, their value of clearance will correspond to $$Cl_i=\theta_{Cl}\times e^{\eta_i}$$ now, it should be noted that $\eta$ is assumed to follow a normal distribution with mean of zero and standard deviation $\omega_{Cl}$ The mathematical notation for this is as follows $$\eta\sim N(0,\omega_{Cl}^2)$$ you will see from this that in a non-linear mixed-effects model, we do not estimate the population mean value of $\eta$ (since it is always zero), we estimate the variance, $\omega^2$. Again, we have to tell our computer program a value to start looking for this. 

You will finally note, that by choosing to model $$Cl=\theta_{Cl}\times e^{\eta_{Cl}}$$ we are modelling clearance with a log-normal distribution. We *could* choose to model it $$Cl=\theta_{Cl}+ \eta_{Cl}$$but as we have previously demonstrated, our *prior* knowledge of clearance is that it is likely to have a log-normal distribution so we should choose to model it as such. 


# Non-linear mixed-effects modelling in <TT>R</TT>  

Non-linear mixed-effects modelling is computationally complex and requires knowledge of statistics, pharmacological models and how to use computer code. We are going to enter into the world of population pharmacokinetic modelling by using the package <TT>nlmixr</TT>. 

Installing <TT>nlmixr</TT> is a little complicated. You *may* be able to do it by simply calling <TT>install.packages(nlmixr2)</TT> or <TT>install.packages(nlmixr2, type="source")</TT>. However it is more likely that you will first need to install [<TT>Rtools</TT>](https://cran.rstudio.com/bin/windows/Rtools/) (if you use a windows machine) or [<TT>Xcode Command Line Tools</TT>](https://www.freecodecamp.org/news/install-xcode-command-line-tools/) (if you are on an Apple Mac). These programs and the <TT>nlmixr</TT> package take up a considerable amount of memory. The install on an Apple Mac can be particularly painful. It is unlikely that I will be able to support all of you to install this on your machines yourself so you will need to try to troubleshoot your own install issues if you choose to install on your own machine. 

## Using <TT>nlmixr</TT>

First you need to call <TT>library(nlmixr2)</TT> to load the package. Then you need to build your model. We will attempt to fit a one-compartment oral-absorption model to the sparse furosemide data. The data set is available on the Advanced PK GitHub and can be called in the usual way using <TT>read\_csv()</TT>.

Then you need to build your model. When building the model, you specify two blocks in a standard <TT>R function()</TT>. As always, you give your function a name, I have chosen <TT>one.cmt</TT>. 

The first part of the function is to set the initial estimates for your model parameters, this is known as the <TT>ini()</TT> block. Remember that we are parameterising using $k_a$, $Cl$ and $V$ in our one-compartment oral-absorption model. So we need to tell <TT>nlmixr</TT> initial values for each of these parameters *as well as* values for the random-effects ($\omega^2$) associate with each parameter. 

The second part of the function defines the model. The <TT>model()</TT> block. Here you describe the relationship between the fixed- and random-effects. A little more on this later. 

After building the model we can run a check using <TT>nlmixr(one.cmt)</TT>, which describes the model we have built. And then we fit..........

```{r nlmixr,warning=F,message=F}

sparse<-
 read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/dlonsdal/SGUL_PK_data/main/sparsefru.csv")
library(nlmixr2)

# First make the model
one.cmt <- function() {
  ini({ 
    tka <-log(1.3) # set the initial estimates for each parameter, note that ka will not be required for IV datasets
    tcl <- log(8) 
    tv <- log(20)
    eta.ka~0.1 # interindividual variabilities
    eta.cl ~ 0.1
    eta.v ~ 0.1
    add.sd <- 0.01 # residual variability
  })
  model({ # define the model
    ka <- exp(tka + eta.ka) # ka will not be required for IV datasets
    cl <- exp(tcl + eta.cl)
    v <- exp(tv + eta.v)
    linCmt() ~ add(add.sd)
  })
}

# Run a check
nlmixr2(one.cmt)

# Fit the data using this model
fit<-nlmixr2(one.cmt,sparse,est="saem", list(print=0))

```

You can run <TT>print(fit)</TT> and you will get an enormous amount of output from your model. Tables  \@ref(tab:nlmixfix) & \@ref(tab:nlmixran) show the fixed and random parameter estimates, along with confidence intervals. Don't worry about the other output for now.
```{r nlmixfix,echo=F,warning=T,message=F}

kbl(fit$par.fixed %>% 
      select(-last_col(),-6),booktabs = T, caption="Fixed effect parameter estimates from Pop-PK analysis of sparse furosemide data")%>%  kable_styling(latex_options =c("striped"))


```

```{r nlmixran,echo=F,warning=T,message=F}
kbl(signif(diag(fit$omegaR),2), caption="Random effects parameter estimates from Pop-PK analysis of sparse furosemide data",booktabs = T,col.names = c(
                           "Estimate"))%>%  kable_styling(latex_options =c("striped"))
```

# Comparing output from the non-linear mixed-effects approach to the naive-pooled approach

Figure \@ref(fig:nlmfit) shows the fit provided by the non-linear mixed-effects approach compared to the naive-pooled approach, which was our preferred of the other modelling approaches to sparse data from earlier in the session. You can see that the non-linear mixed-effects approach provides a superior fit to both absorption and elimination phases compared to the naive-pooled fit. Indeed, it is so close to the 'true' population value, I have had to fade the 'true' line so you can see it properly! Additionally, with the non-linear mixed-effects approach, we retain information on the inter-individual variability of model parameters.


```{r nlmfit,echo=F,eval=T,fig.cap="Concentration-time profile showing \'true\' population profile alonside a variety of approaches to modelling sparse data",fig.width=6,fig.height=3,fig.align = 'center',warning=FALSE,message=FALSE}

sim<-function(x) ((Dose*F*mka)/(mV*(mka-mke)))*(exp(-mke*x)-exp(-mka*x))
np<- function(x) npI*(exp(-npke*x)-exp(-npka*x))
nlmke<-(15.5/18.9)
nlm<-function(x)((Dose*1.33)/(18.9*(1.33-nlmke)))*(exp(-nlmke*x)-exp(-1.33*x))


time<-seq(0,8,by=0.1)
conc<-c(sim(time),np(time),nlm(time))
time<-rep(seq(0,8,by=0.1),3)
data<-rep(c("True (population) PK profile","Naive pooled","Non-linear mixed-effects"),each=81)
data<-factor(data,levels=c("True (population) PK profile","Naive pooled","Non-linear mixed-effects"))

full<-data.frame(data,time,conc)


ggplot()+
  geom_point(data=fru,aes(x=time,y=conc),alpha=0.6,col='grey')+
  geom_line(data=full,aes(x=time,y=conc,lty=data,col=data,alpha=data),size=1)+
  theme_bw(base_size=9)+
  theme(legend.position=c(0.75,0.8),
        legend.background = element_rect(fill = NA),
        legend.title=element_blank(),
        legend.text=element_text(size=10))+
  ylab("Plasma concentration mg/L")+
  xlab("Time (hr)")+
  scale_linetype_manual(values=c("solid", "twodash","dotdash"))+
  scale_alpha_manual(name = "data", values = c(0.4,1,1))+
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Dark2")
```

# Tidying up other nuances with <TT>nlmixr</TT>

## $\mu$ referencing
You will note in the <TT>nlmixr</TT> code that we seem to for no good reason input a '$log(value)$' for our initial parameter estimates and then place them in the exponential domain in our <TT>model()</TT> block of code. This is known as $\mu$ referencing. 

Remember, we are modelling fixed and random effects for our model parameters $$Cl=\theta_{Cl}\times e^{\eta_{Cl}}$$ now, it just so happens that the estimation algorithms work a little better if all of the parameters being estimated are in the exponential domain. We have to write our model with a little mathematical fiddling to achieve this. Recall that $$e^a\times e^b=e^{a+b}\qquad  \textrm{AND}\qquad  e^{log(c)}=c$$ so instead of inputting $$Cl=\theta_{Cl}\times e^{\eta_{Cl}}$$ into our model we instead input $$Cl= e^{log(\theta_{Cl})+\eta_{Cl}}$$ you will have seen in Table \@ref(tab:nlmixfix) that <TT>nlmixr} estimates $log(\theta)$ and then automatically back-transforms the final parameter estimates to the standard domain.

## Residual variability
In our non-linear mixed-effects model, we are more sophisticated about how we model variability, applying $\eta's$ to the model parameters $$Cl=\theta_{Cl}\times e^{\eta_{Cl}}$$ but these random-effects will not explain all of the deviation of the observed data. There will still be some left over, residual, error. So we add a residual error component to our model. 

This is done in the <TT>linCmt() $\sim$ add(add.sd)</TT> line of code. Here we tell <TT>nlmixr</TT> that we wish our residual error to be modelled in an additive fashion, i.e. $$C=\frac{F.D.k_a}{V_d.(k_a-k_e)}.(e^{-k_et}-e^{-k_at})+\epsilon$$ again, we assume this residual error follows a normal distribution with mean $0$ and variance $\sigma^2$ $$\epsilon\sim N(0,\sigma^2)$$ and you will have noticed that we give <TT>nlmixr</TT> a starting value for $\sigma$. 

# Components of a mixed-effects pharmacokinetic model
We have above described two key parts of a pharmacokinetic model. The **structural** model and the **statistical** model. 

## The structural model

The structural model is the underlying equation that describes the mean population trend. In short, this is the compartmental model you have chosen to model the data with. In the example above this was the one-compartment oral-absorption model. $$C=\frac{F.D.k_a}{V_d.(k_a-k_e)}.(e^{-k_et}-e^{-k_at})$$

## The statistical model

The statistical model is how we have chosen to model the parameter- and residual- variability.

Parameter variability you will recall referred to the $\eta$ terms, for example $$Cl=\theta_{Cl}\times e^{\eta_{Cl}}$$ where $$\eta\sim N(0,\omega_{Cl}^2)$$

And our residual error model was the additive $+\epsilon$ where $$\epsilon\sim N(0,\sigma^2)$$

Later in the course we will add a further component - the **covariate model**.
